reasoning_model_1: The model generates the text content 'Mm, this dish tastes really good!' with high content accuracy and very natural prosody. The voice is clear and expressive, conveying enjoyment through the tone of the 'Mm'. However, regarding the specific command to include a 'smack of the lips', the model fails to produce this specific non-linguistic vocalization. The 'Mm' [0:00-0:02] is rendered purely as a prolonged nasal hum, without the characteristic sharp plosive sound of lips parting (a smack) that was requested. While the delivery is pleasant, it misses the core controllable feature constraint.
reasoning_model_2: The model generates the sentence with a distinct non-linguistic sound at the very beginning [0:00-0:01]. Before voicing the 'Hmm/Mm', there is a clear audible artifact resembling a lip smack or a sharp release of the lips, which directly addresses the user's specific instruction. The subsequent speech 'this dish tastes really good' is natural and fits the context of eating. Although the audio fidelity is slightly different from T1, T2 successfully integrates the requested sound effect (the smack) into the flow of speech, fulfilling the core requirement.
model_comparison: The user explicitly requested a 'smack of the lips' to be included in the reading. Model 1 (T1) provided a high-quality, natural reading of the text but completely ignored the instruction to add the specific sound effect; it simply read 'Mm' as a hum. Model 2 (T2), on the other hand, successfully generated a distinct lip-smacking sound at the very beginning of the audio [0:00-0:01] before delivering the line. Since T2 followed the specific paralinguistic instruction while T1 did not, T2 is the clear winner for this feature control task.
